coredata.dc:title: Biomimetic design and synthesis of visible-light-driven g-C3N4 nanotube @polydopamine/NiCo-layered double hydroxides composite photocatalysts for improved photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity

coredata.dc:description: In the practical process of photocatalytic H2 evolution, optimizing the ability of light absorption and charge spatial separation is the top priority for improving the photocatalytic performance. In this study, we elaborately engineer neoteric g-C3N4 nanotube@polydopamine(pDA)/NiCo-LDH (LPC) composite photocatalyst by combining hydrothermal and calcination method. In the LPC composite system, the one-dimensional (1D) g-C3N4 nanotubes with larger specific surface area can afford more active sites and conduce to shorten the charge migration distance, as well as the high-speed mass transfer in the nanotube can accelerate the reaction course. The g-C3N4/NiCo-LDH type-II heterojunction can efficaciously stimulate the spatial separation of photo-produced charge. In addition, pDA as heterojunction metal-free interface mediums can provide multiple action (π-π* electron delocalization effect, adhesive action and photosensitization). The optimized LPC nanocomposite displays about 3.3-fold high photoactivity for H2 evolution compared with the g-C3N4 nanotube under solar light irradiation. In addition, the cycle experiment result shows that the LPC composite photocatalyst possesses superior stability and recyclability. The resultant g-C3N4@pDA/NiCo-LDH composite photocatalyst displays the potential practical application in the field of energy conversion.

Time courses of photocatalytic H2 evolution over different as-prepared samples (A), photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of different as-prepared samples under visible light irradiation (B), wavelength dependent AQE of H2 evolution over LPC (C), stability study of photocatalytic H2 evolution by LPC (D). Error bars are standard deviations from three experiments.

head.ce:title: Biomimetic design and synthesis of visible-light-driven g-C3N4 nanotube @polydopamine/NiCo-layered double hydroxides composite photocatalysts for improved photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the practical process of photocatalytic H2 evolution, optimizing the ability of light absorption and charge spatial separation is the top priority for improving the photocatalytic performance. In this study, we elaborately engineer neoteric g-C3N4 nanotube@polydopamine(pDA)/NiCo-LDH (LPC) composite photocatalyst by combining hydrothermal and calcination method. In the LPC composite system, the one-dimensional (1D) g-C3N4 nanotubes with larger specific surface area can afford more active sites and conduce to shorten the charge migration distance, as well as the high-speed mass transfer in the nanotube can accelerate the reaction course. The g-C3N4/NiCo-LDH type-II heterojunction can efficaciously stimulate the spatial separation of photo-produced charge. In addition, pDA as heterojunction metal-free interface mediums can provide multiple action (π-π* electron delocalization effect, adhesive action and photosensitization). The optimized LPC nanocomposite displays about 3.3-fold high photoactivity for H2 evolution compared with the g-C3N4 nanotube under solar light irradiation. In addition, the cycle experiment result shows that the LPC composite photocatalyst possesses superior stability and recyclability. The resultant g-C3N4@pDA/NiCo-LDH composite photocatalyst displays the potential practical application in the field of energy conversion.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the swift consumption of traditional fossil energy has led to serious energy crisis and environmental pollution problems [1–4]. Hydrogen energy with advantages of zero pollution, high‐energy density and environment protecting is considered to be a promising alternative. Among the sundry strategies for H2 evolution, semiconductor photocatalysts with directly transform of solar energy into H2 has attracted significant attention by virtue of its high efficiency and sustainability [5–6]. The key to vigorously developing photocatalytic technology is to engineer efficient photocatalysts. However, most photocatalysts still face the problems of low utilization efficiency of visible light, easy recombination of charge carriers and lack of active reaction sites [7,8]. Therefore, solving the above problems has become a research hotspot in the field of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution (see Scheme 1. ).

Graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4), two-dimensional organic layered nanomaterials, is deemed as developmental photocatalysts for applications in nitrogen fixation, bacterial disinfection, degrading environmental pollutants and energy conversion by virtue of its suitable electronic band position, superb physicochemical stability, accessible modification, facile manufacturing operation and so on [9,10]. However, owing to the dissatisfactory visible light absorption capacity, swift recombination rate of the photo-induced charge and small specific area, the photocatalytic performance of pure g-C3N4 requires to be further enhanced. Therefore, there are many methods to reform the intrinsic property of g-C3N4, for example, metal/nonmetal doping, loading co-catalyst, morphological engineering, establishing heterojunction and building defects. The photocatalytic H2 evolution process usually happens on the surface of the photocatalysts, so the modified g-C3N4 with nanostructure and individual morphology possesses better photochemical properties. At present, multiple nanostructure morphologies of g-C3N4 have been synthesized, such as nanosheets [11,12], nanotubes [13,14], nanofibers [15,16], spheres [17,18]. Thereinto, the 1D nanotubes g-C3N4 has acquired high attention since the nanotube structure with higher specific surface area can offer abundant reaction sites and conduce to shorten the charge carrier transmission distance, as well as the fast mass transfer in the nanotube can expedite the reaction course [19]. However, the single component photocatalyst is still limited in its ability to increase charge carrier separation. Multiple previous researches have attested that the formation of heterojunction with another adequate semiconductor is a resultful way to boost the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 photocatalysts. For example, Han et al. covers that the Ti3C2 MXene-derived C-doped TiO2/g-C3N4 photocatalyst presents outstanding photocatalytic H2 evolution when the mass ratio of Ti3C2 to g-C3N4 is 10 wt%, which is attributed to the fact that the intimate heterojunction can achieve to hamper the recombination of charge carriers [20]. And Ren et al. synthesize the S-scheme Sb2WO6/g-C3N4 heterojunction photocatalysts via ultrasound-assisted approach has superior activity for photocatalytic ppb-grade NO removal. The S-scheme charge carriers transfer pathway can effectively separate the photo-induced electron and holes [21]. Liu et al. reports that the g-C3N4 modified by N-ZnO to build Z-scheme heterojunction possesses excellent photocatalytic performance for applying in H2 evolution and degradation organic contaminant. The Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalytic mechanism can control availably separation and transfer of interfacial charge carriers [22]. Inspired by this, constructing heterojunctions appears a more preferable medium to boost the efficiency of H2 generation (see Table 1. ).

Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) and its derivatives as a class of extraordinary two-dimensional (2D) layered nanomaterials has aroused extensive concern by right of their high layer charge density along with 2D interlayer space height in field of thermocatalysts, photocatalysts and electrocatalysts, which provides a reasonable path for charge carrier transport. And the layered double hydroxides are solid similar to brucite, comprising of two representative metallic cations (M2+ and M3+), and the octahedron is encircled by oxo bridge and OH groups. The main structural characteristic of the LDH is M2+ and M3+ cations, which are evenly distributed in the hydroxide layer. To complete the process of optimizing the electronic structure of LDH, merely adjust the chemical component of MII/MIII. Some typical LDH nanomaterials include ZnFe-LDH [23], NiCr-LDH [24], CoAl-LDH [25], NiFe-LDH [26], NiCo-LDH [27] and so forth. Among many of the LDHs, the NiCo-LDH with appropriate energy level position can integrate with g-C3N4 to form type-II heterojunction for elevating the spatial separation efficiency of charge. However, the type-II heterojunction is often not enough effective in stimulating charge separation owning to the fact that interface charge transfer driving force is weak. Finding a heterojunction interface medium bridging the two semiconductors to facilitate the effective separation of charge carriers at the type-II heterojunction interface is pivotal.

Common heterojunction metal-free interface mediums are carbonaceous nanomaterials incorporating reduced graphene oxide (rGO), carbon nanotubes, carbon dots etc., which are widespreadly applied in g-C3N4-based type-II heterojunctions to ameliorate the photocatalytic performance based in the synergy of improved light absorption and charge spatial separation. Such as g-C3N4/GO/AgBr [28], g-C3N4 nanosheets/CDs/BiOBr [29] and g-C3N4/Carbon nanotubes/ Ag3PO4 [30]. Lately, the pDA as mussel-inspired biomimetic carbon materials is active in the domain of chemical, biomedical, sensor and battery manufacturing, etc [31–33], because of its biocompatibility, excellent light capture ability, predominant self‐polymerize property (pH = 8.5). As a consequence, the pDA is treat as a feasible heterojunction interface medium to bridge the g-C3N4 nanotube and NiCo-LDH for increasing the H2 evolution amount, which is ascribed to the π - π* electron delocalization effect between g-C3N4 and pDA for impelling the spatial separation of charge and the multiple action of pDA, that is, the adhesive action makes the g-C3N4/NiCo-LDH interface closer for improving the stability of the photocatalysts and photosensitization for making the light absorption capacity better [34].

In this work, the LPC composite photocatalysts is successfully synthesized for applying in photocatalytic hydrogen evolution (PHE). Apparently, the LPC composites with optimum ratio shows superior photocatalytic H2 evolution activity and good stability within 5 h under solar light irradiation. The premium features of LPC are due to the following reasons: (i) g-C3N4 nanotube can offer abundant reaction sites and conduce to shorten the charge carrier transmission distance, as well as the fast mass transfer in the nanotube can expedite the reaction course, (ii) g-C3N4/NiCo-LDH type-II heterojunction can boost the spatial separation of photo-induced charge, (iii) pDA as heterojunction metal-free interface mediums can provide multiple action (electron delocalization effect, adhesive action and photosensitization). Therefore, the LPC photocatalysts proposed in this study furnishes an original way to enhance the PHE activity.

All reagents are directly used as received without further purification. Chloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6·6H2O, AR), Urea (99%, AR), thiourea (99%, AR), tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane hydrochloride (≥99.9%, AR), dopamine hydrochloride (98%), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, AR), nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, AR) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 97%) are purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company, triethanolamine (TEOA, AR) and ethanol (C2H5OH, 95.0%) are all supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., polyethylene-polypropylene-glycol (average Mn ~ 13,000, Mackin), deionized water is used throughout this experiment.

Carbon nitride nanotubes are synthesized by calcining process. 5 g urea, 5 g thiourea and 20 mg Pluronic F127 are put into a large mortar and grinded until there were no obvious large particles and mixed evenly. Pluronic F127 is used as template agent. Then put in the small crucible, sealed with tin foil, calcined in the muff furnace and heated continuously, the first stage at 350 ℃ at 10 ℃ per minute for 1 h, and the second stage at 600 ℃ at 2 ℃ per minute for 3 h, get g-C3N4 nanotubes.

A series of CN@pDA nanotubes is synthesized in the following steps, first, 0.5 g carbonitridation ultrasound is dispersed in 100 mL water, 0.12 g Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris-HCl) is added, stirred for 10 min, 50 mg dopamine hydrochloride is added, the pH is adjusted to 8.5, and the reaction takes 15 min. After the reaction is completed, the products are centrifuged and washed with deionized water for three times and dried at 60 ℃. The reaction time is changed to 15 min and 5 min, respectively. The above steps are repeated, and the product is named CN@pDA-5, CN@pDA-10 and CN@pDA-15.

0.3 g CN@pDA-10 is dispersed in a 100 mL beaker containing 40 mL H2O and sonicated for 20 min. When the sample is evenly dispersed in the beaker, add a certain volume of Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.5 M) and Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (0.5 M) solution to the mixed solution dropwise, and stir for 30 min to the suspension solution evenly. Then adding sodium hydroxide solution dropwise and continue stirring for 2 h. The amount of NaOH (1 M) added is Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.5 M) and Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (0.5 M) (Ni and Co The molar ratio is 1:1) 5 times of the mixed solution. Subsequently, the mixture is kept at 100 ℃ for 24 h in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Centrifuge the as-prepared sample and wash two times with deionized water. The sample is dried in oven at 60 ℃ for 12 h in the end.

In order to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the samples, experiments are conducted in a closed system, using a 300 W Xe-lamp carried with a 420 nm filter to irradiate the sample solution vertically. First of all, 50 mg of photocatalyst powder is ground and added to 100 mL of an aqueous solution containing 20% vol.% TEOA, and a certain amount of H2PtCl6·6H2O (Pt, 3 wt%) solution is added, with TEOA as a sacrifice agent, Pt as cocatalyst. Secondly, degas the hydrogen production system, exhaust the air from the channel and the solution, and then illuminate.Putting to use a gas chromatograph carried with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD, N2 as the carrier gas), the amount of hydrogen produced is measured every hour and the data is recorded five times. The photo-electrochemical performance test of sample uses the same light source as hydrogen evolution. In addition, in the same case, the characteristic quantum yields (AQY) of 400, 420, 450, 500, 550, and 600 nm are studied using bandpass monochromatic filters, respectively.
2.6 The particular characterization methods and photo-electrochemical examination are displayed in Supporting Information

The characteristic crystal structures of g-C3N4 nanotubes, NiCo-LDH nanosheets, CN@pDA, LPC are shown in Fig. 1 A. The g-C3N4 nanotubes exhibited a feeble peak at 12.9° and a strong diffraction at 27.5° corresponding to the (100) and (002) planes [35]. Additionally, the diffraction peaks are located at 19.0, 32.7, 38.2, 51.7, 58.6, 62.0 and 69.6°, corresponding to the (001), (100), (101), (102), (110), (111) and (103) crystal planes of NiCo-LDH phase [36]. It is observed that compared with pure g-C3N4, the peak position of CN@pDA do not change, which indicates that the polymerization reaction of pDA on the surface of g-C3N4 nanotubes hardly any affect the molecular structure of g-C3N4. The characteristic peaks of g-C3N4 and NiCo-LDH are observed in the diffractions of LPC sample, illustrating that the crystal phases of NiCo-LDH and g-C3N4 exists in the LPC, and the characteristic peaks of g-C3N4 nanotubes are decreased due to the doping of NiCo-LDH nanosheets. The FTIR spectra of g-C3N4, NiCo-LDH, CN@pDA and LPC are presented in Fig. 2 B. The typically wide absorption in the region of 3000–3600 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching of OH and NH2, respectively [37]. The sharp peak at 810 cm−1 in the spectrum of g-C3N4 corresponds to the stretching of tri-s-triazine ring, while a series of strong bands in the region of 1200–1700 cm−1 are assigned to CN and CN in the heptazine motifs [38,39]. In addition, the spectrum of CN@pDA is like to that of g-C3N4, which might be attributed to the overlap by the absorptions from g-C3N4. As for NiCo-LDH, the absorption peaks at 3632 and 1051 cm−1 belong to the MO-H (M on behalf of Ni or Co) stretching vibration [40]. In the spectra of LPC composite, the absorption bands of the g-C3N4 decrease, and it is obvious that the characteristic absorptions of NiCo-LDH and g-C3N4 can be found in spectrum of LPC, which further indicates that the feature structural of NiCo-LDH and g-C3N4 are retained in the heterojunction.

The microarchitecture of the resultant g-C3N4 nanotubes, NiCo-LDH nanosheets, CN@pDA, and LPC are observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM). As depicted in Fig. 3 A, the stacked g-C3N4 nanotubes have the appearance of mostly open ends and slightly curved, with the length range of 4.0–6.0 μm and diameter of 0.5–1.0 μm. The wall of the g-C3N4 nanotubes is pretty thin like a gauze, with hollow interior and wrinkled surface (Fig. 3B-C). Tubular g-C3N4 is curly formed by g-C3N4 nanosheets in the continuous high-temperature calcination process with “F127″ as the template. Therefore, we can also observe from Fig. 3C that a little number of g-C3N4 nanosheets are connected to the outside of the tube wall. As displayed in Fig. 3D, it can be legibly observed that the color of g-C3N4 nanotubes deepens, and the wrinkles on the nanotube surface are reduced and tends to be smooth, which is stemmed from the fact that the self-polymerization of dopamine on the surface and inside of g-C3N4 nanotubes to form polydopamine layer under alkaline conditions [41]. And the NiCo-LDH nanosheets present regular hexagonal shape, with sizes between 100 and 140 nm (Fig. 2E) and thickness of 3.0 nm, which is verified by AFM (Fig. S1) [42,43]. The Fig. 2F displays the morphology of the LPC ternary photocatalytic material, the region circled by the yellow dotted line in the figure is the NiCo-LDH nanosheets. It can be observed that the NiCo-LDH nanosheets is attached to the surface and edge of CN@pDA nanotubes, and presents the characteristics of multi-density. According to HRTEM images (Fig. 2G), the NiCo-LDH exhibits the lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.25 nm, pertaining to (012) lattice plane [27], which substantiates that the LPC nanocomposite includes NiCo-LDH nanosheets and the they are in close contact. Also, the composition and distribution of the elements of the LPC are disclosed by element mapping (Fig. 2H), the C, N, Ni, Co and O elements are homogeneously distributed (Fig. 2H). From the above analysis, it can adequately unravel that the LPC photocatalysts has been successfully synthesized [44,45].

The XPS spectra are gathered to evaluate the elemental composition and chemical environment of the g-C3N4, NiCo-LDH and LPC. As depicted in Fig. 3A, LPC composite photocatalyst is composed of all elements contained in g-C3N4 and NiCo-LDH, namely C, N, O, Ni and Co elements. According to XPS spectrum in Fig. 3B, the N 1 s spectra of g-C3N4 and LPC both can be divided into three characteristic peaks, the characteristic peaks of LPC at 401.1, 400.1, 389.9 eV are corresponded to CNH, N-(C)3 and C-NC, severally [46]. By comparing with the binding energy of LPC, it is found that the secondary peak binding energy of pure g-C3N4 has shifted to different degrees, which may be caused by the interaction between different components. Deconvolution of C1s spectrum of LPC (Fig. 3C) displays four characteristic peaks, which are located at 288.4, 286.2, 285.0, 284.5 eV and assign to C-(N)3, CO, CN and CC, respectively. Similarly, the pure g-C3N4 also has three characteristic peaks. Compared with the CO peak of pure g-C3N4, the peak area of the CO peak in the LPC composite is significantly increased, which may be because this peak also contains the CO peak in pDA. Therefore, it further proves that pDA is contained in LPC [47,48].

The Ni 2p spectra of LPC are made up of two spin–orbit doublets and two shakeup satellites (Fig. 3D). Especially, the peaks at 873.1 and 855.5 eV are attributed to 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 of Ni2+, respectively, while the peaks at 874.7 and 856.5 eV are attributed to 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 of Ni3+. The two shakeup satellites peaks situate at 861.4 and 879.4 eV. Analogously, as illustrated in Fig. 3E, the LPC possesses double valent statess pin-orbits, the characteristic peaks at 781.9 and 798.3 eV are correspond to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 orbit of Co2+, respectively, and the characteristic peaks at 780.4 and 796.5 eV correspond to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 orbit of Co3+, respectively. In addition, there are two peaks located at 784.7 and 803.9 eV, which are assigned to the satellite peaks. The Ni 2p and Co 2p spectra of LPC are similar to the characteristic peaks of pure NiCo-LDH. Compared with LPC, the characteristic peaks of NiCo-LDH are also shifted. Above results adequately indicate the LPC composite are successfully prepared.

The photocatalytic H2 evolution activity of resultant samples is researched and the experimental results are displayed in Fig. 4 . The experiments on the photocatalytic H2 evolution of all samples are conducted under the excitation of visible light (λ > 420 nm) and triethanolamine (TEOA) is chosen as the capture reagent of the holes. As depicted in Fig. 4A, it is visibly seen that the H2 evolution capacity of sole NiCo-LDH is negligible. Unsurprisingly, the photocatalytic activity of pure g-C3N4 is also unsatisfactory, and its hydrogen production is only 58.1 μmol in 5 h, which is mainly imputed to the fact that the visible light absorption capability of single-component photocatalytic material is insufficient and the photo-generated charge carriers are inclined to rapidly recombine. In order to overcome the above disadvantages, pDA has been loaded on the surface of the g-C3N4 nanotubes, the H2 evolution activity of g-C3N4 is ameliorated conspicuously and the H2 evolution amount is 121.3 μmol. The increased amount of H2 evolution due to the following two major factors, first of all, the delocalization effect between pDA and g-C3N4 can furnish driving force for separation and migration of charge carriers. Secondly, the pDA also possesses superior electron transport capability, pDA is tightly wrapped on the surface of g-C3N4 nanotubes along with larger contact area between them, which affords abundant channels for charge carrier transfer and availably controls charge carrier recombination [49]. Not only that, the photocatalytic activity of LDH/CN (292.6 μmol) is also tested to validate that the NiCo-LDH is a popular photocatalytic material in the photocatalytic field, its energy band position is befitting to g-C3N4 to form type-II heterojunction, which further accelerates charge carrier separation and improves evidently photocatalytic H2 evolution activity. The resultant sample of LPC displays the highest H2 evolution activity of 398.7 μmol, which is about 1.4 folds higher than that of LDH/CN. The H2 evolution amount of g-C3N4, CN@pDA, LDH/CN and LPC change linearly with the extension of light time, substantiating that hydrogen production can be continuous within 5 h. As displayed in 4B, the HER rate of pure NiCo-LDH is trace under visible light irradiation, the pure g-C3N4, CN@pDA LDH/CN and LPC are 232.5, 485.4, 1170.5 and 1555.1 μmol h-1g−1, respectively. By contrast, obviously, LPC has the highest HER (1555.1 μmol h-1g−1). Therefore, LPC can boost the efficient utilization the solar energy and the photocatalytic performance.

The apparent quantum efficiencies (AQE) is explored by altering filters (400–550 nm) with diverse wavelengths under the same situation (Fig. 4C), and the calculated maximum quantum effiency is approximately 7.5% in the wavelength of 400 nm, which is well coincided with light absorption edges, suggesting that the the evolution of H2 is related to light induction property. The stability of a photocatalyst is a significant element in determining its shelf-life and efficiency. To evaluate the stability of the photocatalyst, the test cycle is performed in triethanolamine (TEOA) and the results are observed in Fig. 4D. In the 20 h continuous cycle test, the H2 precipitation of LPC samples decreased slightly, but there was no obvious degradation, indicating that LPC had a high stability for H2 precipitation in TEOA solution. Meantime, the sample are further characterized by XRD after the cycle test (Fig. S2), and there is no significant change compared with the previous sample, which again proves the stability of the LPC photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photoelectrochemical properties and charge transfer dynamics
ce:section.ce:para: The optical absorption characteristics of the four photocatalysts are studied by UV–vis diffuse reflection (DRS). It is generally known that the photocatalytic performance of photocatalysts is related to its light absorption capacity. As displayed in Fig. 5 A, the light absorption edges appear in 446, 455 and 465 nm, respectively, for g-C3N4, CN@pDA and LPC. At the same time, NiCo-LDH is absorbed in the range of 200–800 nm, which may be related to its black color. Compared with single-component g-C3N4 nanotubes, the light absorption edge of CN@pDA is redshifted, which may be caused by the darkening and black color of the sample after pDA attachment to the surface of g-C3N4 nanotubes [50]. Hence, it is not difficult to discover that pDA as photosensitizer wrapping g-C3N4 nanotubes effectively promotes the light acquisition capability of photocatalysts. In addition, the LPC possesses the maximum light absorption edge. It ascertains that the integration of NiCo-LDH, pDA and g-C3N4 apparently broadens the response range of visible light, which is benefited to heighten the photocatalytic H2 evolution efficiency.

EIS is usually used to investigate the electron recombination efficiency and interfacial charge transport behavior. Fig. 5B shows the Nyquist diagram of g-C3N4, NiCo-LDH, CN@pDA, and LPC samples, which reflects the charge transfer ability at the photocatalyst/electrolyte interface, and the smaller radius corresponds to the lower resistance [51]. In accordance with expectation that the arc radius of the optimized LPC electrodes is obviously smaller than those of g-C3N4, NiCo-LDH, CN@pDA electrodes, unraveling that LPC has a lower charge carriers transfer drag and the effective spatial charge separation is realized through charge robust interfacial immigration between g-C3N4 and NiCo-LDH intimated heterojunction. In addition, the pDA as electron transfer channel can expedite electrons motion [52].

In order to characterize the photo-generated charge carrier separation and migration efficiency of the photocatalyst, the pure g-C3N4, NiCo-LDH, CN@pDA and LPC sample is tested and analyzed by transient photocurrent response under repeated On/Off light illumination cycles (Fig. 5C). Generally, the stronger the photocurrent response of the photocatalyst, the faster the interface charge separation and migration efficiency [53–54]. Compared with pure g-C3N4 and NiCo-LDH, the photocurrent density of CN@pDA and LPC are sequentially enhanced. More importantly, the formation of heterojunction between NiCo-LDH and g-C3N4 can further modulate the surface reaction kinetics and make a qualitative leap in the photocatalytic H2 evolution activity of LPC, which stems from the fact that synergistic effect of pDA as electronic transmission medium and heterojunction structure providing charge carrier driving force can jointly impell efficient electrons transfer [55]. Moreover, after 13 On/Off photoperiod, the LPC possesses satisfactory reversible and reproducible, unveiling that its good photoelectrochemical stability. Furthermore, the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of the samples is displayed in Fig. 5D. The overpotential of NiCo-LDH, g-C3N4, CN@pDA and LPC at 40 mA cm−2 is 0.51, 0.49, 0.16 and 0.43 V, respectively, indicating that LPC with the lower overpotential can dramatically elevates the proton reduction to H2 [56].

The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectrum is a common method to inquiry the excited states of photocatalyst materials, which can supply pivotal information about the arrest and recombination of charge carriers for PL emission produced by the recombination of free photo-generated electrons and holes [57]. The PL spectrum of g-C3N4, CN@pDA, and LPC display different peaks under excitation at 337 nm (Fig. 5E), which in demonstrates there is intrinsical difference in photophysical characteristics of them. Due to the rapid recombination of photo-generated charge carriers, single-component g-C3N4 shows the highest peak. Subsequently, the peak value decreased via integrating pDA and g-C3N4, which is attributed to the fact that the pDA as electronic transmission medium can availably expedite electrons transfer and hamper the recombination of electron-holes pairs [58]. The heterojunction structure formed by g-C3N4 and LPC further improves the carrier separation efficiency, so the LPC possesses the lowest emission peaks [59]. Simultaneously, applying the time-resolved photoluminescence spectrum can obtain (formula:

) average launch life of samples (Fig. 5F) [60]. Which can conclude that the average fluorescence life of LPC (6.4 ns) is higher than that of single-component g-C3N4 (3.4 ns), and the average emission life of CN@pDA (3.9 ns). Above result further explains that pDA played an excellent role in electrons transport between g-C3N4 and NiCo-LDH and effectively expedites electron-hole pairs transport, which is to the benefit of the formation of H2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Other influence factors of PHE activity
ce:section.ce:para: The specific surface area and pore information of as-prepared photocatalysts are carefully investigated because most of the chemical reactions in solution occur on the surface of the catalyst [61,62]. Generally speaking, the photocatalysts with larger the specific surface area can furnish more reactive sites to boost the photocatalytic hydrogen production performance. As shown in Fig. 6 A, all samples abide by the typical IV curves, which proves that the resultant samples have a mesoporous structure. The specific surface area of g-C3N4, NiCo-LDH, CN@pDA and LPC is 58.6, 6.7, 68.1, 165.0 m2/g. Obviously, the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 increases as it is modified step by step, and it coincides with the order of increasing specific surface area [63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible photocatalytic mechanism of PHE
ce:section.ce:para: As we all know, the superior performance of photocatalytic hydrogen production is intently correlative to the effective separation and migration of carriers. Hence, we need to make sure the energy band position of g-C3N4 and NiCo-LDH through Mott-Schottky and valence band XPS spectra (VB-XPS) to summarize the carrier migration state [64]. As depicted in Fig. 7 A-B, the valence band (VB) positions of g-C3N4 and NiCo-LDH are calculated to be 1.53 and 1.82 eV, respectively. And the Fig. 7C and D show the Mott-Schottky plots of g-C3N4 and NiCo-LDH in the range of from 1.0 to 2.0 kHz. The metric positive trends signify that BCN and DCN350 are both n‐type semiconductors (pH = 7.0). In addition, considering the high doping density of the sample calculated from the Mott-Schottky curve, the conduction band (CB) level is equivalent to the flat band position. So the flat band position from the x‐intercepts in the corresponding M‐S plots for BCN and DCN350 are −0.94 and −0.47 eV versus the conventional hydrogen electrode (NHE), respectively.

On the basis of acquired information, a rational mechanism of photocatalytic H2 evolution process on the LPC are displayed in Scheme 2 . Both g-C3N4 and NiCo-LDH can effectively come into being electrons and holes under visible light irradiation due to the narrow band gap. Meanwhile, the pDA as photosensitizer wrapping g-C3N4 nanotubes can effectively facilitate the light absorption ability, which can generate more charge carriers in the catalytic system to participate in the photocatalytic hydrogen production reaction. Not only that, the pDA as interface medium bridging the g-C3N4 and NiCo-LDH can facilitate the effective transmission of charge carriers at the type-II heterojunction interface. The produced electrons at CB of g-C3N4 can migrate to the CB of NiCo-LDH owning to its higher energy level. Simultaneously, the holes transfer from the VB of NiCo-LDH to the VB of g-C3N4 [65,66]. Whereafter, a good deal of electrons assembles in the CB of NiCo-LDH to react with H+ in the H2O via the co-catalyst Pt nanoparticles, the holes with stronger oxidation ability in the VB of g-C3N4 can be consumed by the sacrificial reagent TEOA in the aqueous solution, thereby further boosting the photocatalytic H2 evolution performance [67–68]. Therefore, the effective separation and transfer of charge carriers is completed under the synergy of type-II heterojunction and pDA with multiple action (π-π* electron delocalization effect, adhesive action and photosensitization), which has great advantages compared to monomers g-C3N4 and NiCo-LDH.

In summary, the g-C3N4@pDA/NiCo-LDH (LPC) type-II heterojunction photocatalyst with fine photocatalytic H2 evolution performance is prepared by combining calcination with hydrothermal means for applying in energy conversion domain. Compared with the traditional g-C3N4, the synergetic strategy of biomass material (pDA) and NiCo-LDH proposed in this work effectively improves its photocatalytic hydrogen production performance. The optimized LPC composite has highest photocatalytic activity, the H2 evolution rate of the LPC (1555.1 μmol h-1g−1) is approximately 6.7 and 3.2 times high than that of g-C3N4 (232.5 μmol h-1g−1) and CN@pDA (485.4 μmol h-1g−1), respectively. The significantly ameliorative photocatalytic activity of H2 evolution under visible light exposure is chiefly ascribed to the fact that g-C3N4 nanotube can offer abundant reaction sites and conduce to shorten the charge carrier transmission distance, as well as the fast mass transfer in the nanotube can expedite the reaction course, and g-C3N4/NiCo-LDH type-II heterojunction can boost the spatial separation of photo-induced charge, as well as pDA as heterojunction metal-free interface mediums can provide multiple action (electron delocalization effect, adhesive action and photosensitization). Therefore, the LPC photocatalysts has excellent photocatalytic properties. In addition, the LPC also displays good photocatalytic H2 evolution stability. This study furnishes a new pathway to form of high activity and low expense photocatalysts.
